title: Repairing a hollow-sounding floor tile
subtitle: Re-bond a debonded tile before it cracks and before water gets underneath
excerpt: How to diagnose and repair a floor tile that sounds hollow when tapped; indicating the tile has debonded from its adhesive bed. Covers injection repair for intact tiles and full lift-and-relay for cracked or repeatedly failing tiles.
sourceNotes: BS 5385-3: Wall and floor tiling; code of practice for the design and installation of internal floor tiling (domestic). CTDA (Ceramic Tile Distributors Association) repair guidance. City and Guilds Level 2 Tiling.

glossaryTerms.definition: The separation of a tile from its adhesive bed, caused by insufficient adhesive coverage, thermal movement, substrate settlement, or water ingress weakening the bond over time. A debonded tile sounds hollow when tapped because there is an air void beneath it. The tile itself may be intact; the bond between tile and adhesive has failed.

A low-viscosity epoxy or polyurethane resin injected into the void beneath a debonded tile through small holes drilled through the grout joint. The resin flows into the void, bonds to both the tile back and the adhesive bed, and cures hard. Allows a hollow tile to be re-bonded without lifting it — provided the tile is still intact.

content.text: A floor tile that sounds hollow when tapped has

from its adhesive bed: an air void has formed underneath. Left in place, the tile flexes underfoot, the grout joints crack, and water ingress accelerates the failure of surrounding tiles. An intact hollow tile can often be repaired without lifting it, using a
fed through the grout joint. A cracked tile must be lifted and re-laid.

For injection repair: low-viscosity tile injection resin (specialist tile merchants and online suppliers). A 6 mm masonry drill bit. Replacement grout. For full lift-and-relay: a grout rake or oscillating multi-tool. Flexible tile adhesive. A replacement tile (if the original is cracked). A notched trowel. Grout and grout float. Tile spacers.

Tap across the tile with a coin or the handle of the utility knife. A dull thud is a solid bond; a hollow drum-like ring is a void. Map the void: tap in a grid to find its extent. A small, localised void (under less than half the tile area) is a candidate for injection repair. A void across the whole tile, a cracked tile, or a tile with cracked or broken grout on all four sides needs to be lifted and re-laid.
Step 2, injection repair for intact tiles

Rake out 20 to 30 mm of grout from the joint nearest the void using the grout rake or utility knife tip. Drill a 6 mm hole into the joint at an angle to reach the void; the drill must penetrate the adhesive bed below the tile, not just the tile itself. Inject the low-viscosity resin into the hole, moving slowly to allow it to flow into the void. Inject from multiple holes around the perimeter of the void if needed. Wipe off excess resin from the tile face immediately with a cloth dampened with the recommended solvent (check the product data sheet). Allow the resin to cure per the manufacturer's instructions; typically 4 to 6 hours. Re-grout the joint once the resin has fully set.
Step 3, full lift-and-relay for cracked or large-void tiles

Rake out the grout from all four joints around the tile. Drill a series of holes through the tile face to weaken it, then break it out in sections using a cold chisel and hammer; protect surrounding tiles with strips of masking tape. Remove all adhesive from the substrate using the SDS drill with a chisel bit. The substrate must be clean and flat before new adhesive is applied. Lay the replacement tile (or the original if undamaged) in flexible adhesive, back-buttering as well as applying adhesive to the substrate. Check it is level with the surrounding tiles, insert spacers, and allow to cure for 24 hours before grouting.

attrs.symptom: Injection resin did not reach the full extent of the void
attrs.cause: Resin too viscous, or only one injection point used.
attrs.fix: Drill additional holes at 50 mm intervals around the void perimeter and inject from each. Allow resin to flow for 2 to 3 minutes per hole before moving to the next.

attrs.symptom: Tile debonds again within months after re-laying
attrs.cause: Substrate movement or insufficient adhesive coverage on the tile back.
attrs.fix: Check the substrate for movement (a soft or springy subfloor needs to be investigated before re-tiling). Ensure full adhesive coverage by back-buttering the tile in addition to the floor bed.
